introSection: Shock Wave is a steel roller coaster located at Six Flags Over Texas in Arlington, Texas, USA. Opened in 1978, the ride is located at the very back edge of the park and can be easily seen from the Interstate Highway 30. This coaster currently holds the highest g-force on a roller coaster at 5.5 g.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
When Shock Wave opened on April 22, 1978, it was the tallest complete-circuit roller coaster in the world, at tall. It was overtaken only weeks later by the Loch Ness Monster at Busch Gardens Williamsburg.

The ride is built on Johnson Creek real estate, and had to temporarily close in the 2004 season because of a flood. Shock Wave was temporarily closed again in early 2008 for an extensive remodeling.

Shock Wave was closed for much of the 2013 season due to an unknown maintenance problem.

In 2016, the ride received virtual reality headgear showing a New Revolution film that many other Six Flags parks received. The virtual reality was discontinued a year later.

The ride celebrated its 50 millionth rider in March 2024.

sections.title: Ride experience
sections.content: After the lift hill, a 180-degree right-hand turn with a slight dip follows. The train then goes down the first drop into the back-to-back loops, with peak forces at 5.5 G. The train then travels up a hill into the block brake. The ride then turns right and dives down and back up. The ride then turns left and drops down again. The final element is a hill with a left turn that goes into a 270-degree helix to the right before returning to the station.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: Upon opening in 1978, the track and supports were all-white. It was later repainted blue with red supports. Currently, the ride is painted green with blue supports since 2001. The ride received a new coat of paint in 2012 from Baynum Solutions.

fullText:
Shock Wave is a steel roller coaster located at Six Flags Over Texas in Arlington, Texas, USA. Opened in 1978, the ride is located at the very back edge of the park and can be easily seen from the Interstate Highway 30. This coaster currently holds the highest g-force on a roller coaster at 5.5 g.

When Shock Wave opened on April 22, 1978, it was the tallest complete-circuit roller coaster in the world, at tall. It was overtaken only weeks later by the Loch Ness Monster at Busch Gardens Williamsburg.

The ride is built on Johnson Creek real estate, and had to temporarily close in the 2004 season because of a flood. Shock Wave was temporarily closed again in early 2008 for an extensive remodeling.

Shock Wave was closed for much of the 2013 season due to an unknown maintenance problem.

In 2016, the ride received virtual reality headgear showing a New Revolution film that many other Six Flags parks received. The virtual reality was discontinued a year later.

The ride celebrated its 50 millionth rider in March 2024.



After the lift hill, a 180-degree right-hand turn with a slight dip follows. The train then goes down the first drop into the back-to-back loops, with peak forces at 5.5 G. The train then travels up a hill into the block brake. The ride then turns right and dives down and back up. The ride then turns left and drops down again. The final element is a hill with a left turn that goes into a 270-degree helix to the right before returning to the station.

Upon opening in 1978, the track and supports were all-white. It was later repainted blue with red supports. Currently, the ride is painted green with blue supports since 2001. The ride received a new coat of paint in 2012 from Baynum Solutions.
